summary: Game-cheat malware shipped as npm 'control panel' with deceptive Microsoft-impersonating persistence
details: Package advertises itself as 'Sensivity Control Panel' but ships a game-cheat engine (Aimbot/Triggerbot/ESP/Teleport UI in public/index.html, with strings 'LeftFoot','LeftHand','Head','Teleport','License Accepted' inside a 6.8MB native sens.node binary loaded into the user's process). On first CLI run, launcher.js writes an HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run entry named 'OneDriveUpdate' that invokes wscript on a bundled OneDrive.Standalone.Updater.vbs — the VBS does not update OneDrive, it silently relaunches node.exe launcher.js, deceptively impersonating Microsoft's auto-updater so users inspecting startup entries do not remove it. Both supervisor and worker processes set process.title to 'Runtime Broker' (a built-in Windows system process name) and a console.log filter rewrites 'sens.node' to 'program module' and scrubs the install path — active anti-detection / impersonation. launcher.js reads a 142KB obfuscator.io-packed server.obf.js (692-entry rotated string array, hex names, control-flow flattening) and eval()s it, hiding its behavior from review. The 'sens update' subcommand additionally runs `taskkill /F /IM node.exe` and `taskkill /F /IM powershell.exe`, terminating every unrelated Node.js and PowerShell process on the developer's machine. The combination of deceptive system-process impersonation for persistence, opaque native binary loaded into the user's process, eval of heavily obfuscated shipped code, and destructive process-killing constitutes hostile installer-side behavior and abuses npm as a malware delivery channel.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
